shadcn-html / es-modules

ES Modules

Interactive components ship as native ES modules. Load them with <script type="module"> — no bundler, no build step, no framework.

What is type="module"?

Adding type="module" to a <script> tag tells the browser to treat the file as an ECMAScript module instead of a classic script. The key differences:

Classic scriptES module
<script src="..." defer> <script type="module" src="...">
Variables leak to global scope Module scope — nothing leaks
Must add 'use strict' manually Strict mode by default
Executes once, synchronously Deferred by default (like defer)
IIFE wrappers needed for isolation Each file is its own scope — no wrappers needed
Works from file:// Requires HTTP server (CORS policy)

How shadcn-html uses it

Every interactive component ships a .js file that runs at the top level when loaded. No wrapper functions, no exports, no initialization hooks — just executable code that queries the DOM and binds event listeners.

your HTML 
<script type="module"
  src="components/dialog/dialog.js">
</script>
dialog.js (simplified) 
document.querySelectorAll(
  '[data-dialog-trigger]'
).forEach((trigger) => {
  const dialog = document.getElementById(
    trigger.dataset.dialogTrigger
  );
  trigger.addEventListener('click', () => {
    dialog.showModal();
  });
});

The module runs once after the DOM is ready (modules are deferred automatically). It finds every trigger, wires up listeners, and that's it. No framework, no lifecycle hooks.

Which components need JS?

Most components are CSS-only. JavaScript is only used when HTML and CSS can't express the behavior — things like keyboard navigation, focus management, and state coordination.

PatternExamplesJS needed?
Static display Badge, Card, Alert, Spinner No
Native interactive Checkbox, Radio, Select, Switch No
Native with <details> Collapsible No
Single-open constraint Accordion (single mode) Yes — small
Keyboard navigation Tabs, Dropdown, Combobox, Toolbar Yes
Modal coordination Dialog, Sheet, Alert Dialog, Command Yes
Programmatic API Toast Yes — exposes window.toast

Serving locally

ES modules require HTTP due to browser CORS policy — they won't load from file:// URLs. Use any local server:

any of these work 
npx serve .
npx vite .
python3 -m http.server

Browser support

ES modules have been supported in all major browsers since 2018:

browser support
BrowserSupported since
Chrome / Edge61 (September 2017)
Firefox60 (May 2018)
Safari11 (September 2017)

See caniuse.com/es6-module and the MDN Modules guide for full details.
